Document [16] (Title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- Decayed-Missing-Filled Index) The DMFT score for an individual will range from 0 to 28. A score of 0 means no teeth are decayed, missing, or filled. A score of 28 means that all teeth are affected. If a tooth or surface is both restored and decayed, it is quantified as decayed. The DMFS score for an individual will range from 0 to 128, as anterior teeth have 4 surfaces and molars and premolars have 5 surfaces. Missing or crowned teeth pose a problem to the DMFS; controversy exists regarding how many surfaces should be counted as missing or restored in such teeth. [10] Assigning the maximum value of surfaces may overestimate the caries experience of the individual; similarly, giving a lower value may underestimate the scope of the problem. [10] The 1939 revision of the DMF recommends assigning 3 surfaces to missing and crowned teeth, as three is the most common number of dental surfaces affected by caries in extracted teeth. [10]

Document [26] (Title: Dental Implants -- Clinical Significance) People are living longer on average. This fact, combined with an existing population of patients with minor and major dental problems, guarantees the future of implant dentistry for several generations of dentists. Dental implants are increasingly used to replace single teeth, especially in the posterior regions of the mouth. Rather than removing sound tooth structure and crowning two or more teeth, increasing the risk of decay, endodontic therapy, and splinting teeth together with pontics, which may have the potential to decrease oral hygiene ability and increase plaque retention, a dental implant may replace the single tooth.

Query: Main cause of RPD failure is:

Options:
A: Improper clasp design

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1447) Cerclage (procedure) and REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR are both clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم. قصور عنق الرحم, also known as cervical insufficiency, is a condition in which the cervix is unable to maintain its structural integrity during pregnancy, increasing the risk of premature dilation and preterm delivery. The cerclage procedure involves placing a suturing stitch around the cervix to reinforce it and prevent premature opening, thereby directly addressing the mechanical weakness characteristic of قصور عنق الرحم. Additionally, a poor reproductive history—such as prior miscarriages, preterm births, or other adverse pregnancy outcomes—has been linked to a higher likelihood of developing قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that past obstetric complications can serve as a clinical indicator for this cervical disorder. Recognizing both the therapeutic role of cerclage and the predictive value of reproductive history is essential for early identification and management of patients at risk for قصور عنق الرحم.

Document [12] (Title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2497) Cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم) is a condition in which the uterine cervix is unable to maintain closure during pregnancy, often leading to second‑trimester loss. Recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد) is identified as a parent or underlying factor linked to cervical insufficiency, underscoring the need to assess cervical competence in patients with habitual abortion. The cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency as a therapeutic intervention that reinforces the cervix to prevent premature dilation and improve pregnancy outcomes. Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with cervical insufficiency, indicating that cervical competence may influence the decision‑making and management of planned pregnancy termination. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose, counsel, and manage women at risk for cervical insufficiency and its related obstetric complications.
